--- title: 'On the Urban Renewal of Hayes Valley' published: 2026-06-07 description: 'I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like, but it has always felt a bit manufactured. The history of the neighborhood explains why.' revisionHistory: '/artifacts/on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/revision-history.json' tags: - 'San Francisco' - 'Cities' author: 'Patrick Erichsen' --- import rivianChavez from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/rivian-r2-cesar-chavez-mural.jpg' import armstrongMurals from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/louis-armstrong-ella-fitzgerald-murals.jpg' import centralFreeway from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/central-freeway-hayes-gough-1991.jpg' import RevisionHistoryDrawer from '@components/RevisionHistoryDrawer'

AI Slop Disclosure

This article was generated by AI, but lightly edited by me.

I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. People climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. I didn't think much about why until I read into the neighborhood's history. ## The Urban Renewal of Hayes Valley When I moved here I didn't know much about the neighborhood's history. It turns out a lot of what I notice now goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north of me were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." I can't confirm the name, but into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. People drove through Hayes Valley or stopped to buy something; not many wanted to live in it. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and the Hayes Valley I bike through now grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. I recognize the pattern from home. In South Minneapolis, the Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. It is also the neighborhood where George Floyd was killed in 2020. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. That is the part I keep thinking about. We are about to remake a lot of the world with AI, and much faster than we built freeways. Hayes Valley is a small reminder that these decisions last a lot longer than a quarter.

AI Slop Disclosure

This article was generated by AI, but lightly edited by me.

I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, there are plenty of good restaurants, coffee shops, and bars, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. Tech yuppies climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. Some of that feeling starts to make more sense when you look at the neighborhood's history. ## The Urban Renewal of Hayes Valley When I moved here I didn't know much about the neighborhood's history. It turns out a lot of what I notice now goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north of me were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." I can't confirm the name, but into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. People drove through Hayes Valley or stopped to buy something; not many wanted to live in it. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and the Hayes Valley I bike through now grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. I recognize the pattern from home. In South Minneapolis, the Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. It is also the neighborhood where George Floyd was killed in 2020. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. That is the part I keep thinking about. We are about to remake a lot of the world with AI, and much faster than we built freeways. Hayes Valley is a small reminder that these decisions last a lot longer than a quarter.

I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, there are plenty of good restaurants, coffee shops, and bars, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. Tech yuppies climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. As I've learned about the history of the neighborhood, I've come to better understand and appreciate how things got this way. ## How It Got This Way A lot of what I notice now goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north of me were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." I can't confirm the name, but into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. People drove through Hayes Valley or stopped to buy something; not many wanted to live in it. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and the Hayes Valley I bike through now grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. I recognize the pattern from home. In South Minneapolis, the Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. It is also the neighborhood where George Floyd was killed in 2020. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. That is the part I keep thinking about. We are about to remake a lot of the world with AI, and much faster than we built freeways. Hayes Valley is a small reminder that these decisions last a lot longer than a quarter.

This article was generated by AI, but lightly edited by me.

I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, there are plenty of good restaurants, coffee shops, and bars, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. Tech yuppies climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. As I've learned about the history of the neighborhood, I've come to better understand and appreciate how things got this way. ## The Old Neighborhood A lot of what is visible today goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." Into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and today's Hayes Valley grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. That history made me think about South Minneapolis, where I grew up. The Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. It is also the neighborhood where George Floyd was killed in 2020. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. That is the part I keep thinking about. We are about to remake a lot of the world with AI, and much faster than we built freeways. Hayes Valley is a small reminder that these decisions last a lot longer than a quarter.

It is an easy place to like, but it has always felt a bit manufactured. The history of the neighborhood explains why.' revisionHistory: '/artifacts/on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/revision-history.json' tags: - 'San Francisco' - 'Cities' author: 'Patrick Erichsen' --- import rivianChavez from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/rivian-r2-cesar-chavez-mural.jpg' import armstrongMurals from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/louis-armstrong-ella-fitzgerald-murals.jpg' import centralFreeway from './on-the-urban-renewal-of-hayes-valley/central-freeway-hayes-gough-1991.jpg' import RevisionHistoryDrawer from '@components/RevisionHistoryDrawer'

This article was generated by AI, but lightly edited by me.

I have lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, there are plenty of good restaurants, coffee shops, and bars, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. Tech yuppies climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. As I've learned about the history of the neighborhood, I've come to better understand and appreciate how things got this way. ## The Old Neighborhood A lot of what is visible today goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." Into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and today's Hayes Valley grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. That history made me think about South Minneapolis (I grew up in the suburbs of Minneapolis). The Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. Decades later, George Floyd was killed in that same neighborhood. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. Hayes Valley is a reminder of the many decades it can take for the full implications of policy decisions like urban renewal to play out.

This article was generated by AI, but lightly edited by me.

I've lived in Hayes Valley for about a year and a half. It is an easy place to like. It is one of the sunnier parts of the city, the streets are full of Victorians, there are plenty of good restaurants, coffee shops, and bars, and it is a flat, short bike ride to a lot of good neighborhoods, including the Mission. Still, something about it has always felt a bit manufactured. It reminds me of [SoDoSoPa](https://southpark.fandom.com/wiki/SodoSopa), the fake-trendy development from _South Park_. In Hayes Valley everything is a bit too new and nice and trendy, between the Rivian showroom, local SF chains like Souvla, and boutique outdoor clothing stores like Cotopaxi and Topo. If you sit in Patricia's Green, the small park in the middle of the neighborhood, you can watch the whole place go by. Fresh-faced new grads talk shop about AI. Tech yuppies climb into Waymos holding Marine Layer bags. There is a group from the Castro at the south end, and there are homeless people asleep on the benches. Everyone shares the same park, but nobody really mixes. As I've learned about the history of the neighborhood, I've come to better understand and appreciate how things got this way. ## The Old Neighborhood A lot of what is visible today goes back about seventy years. Hayes Valley is on the south edge of the Western Addition, which used to be one of the main Black neighborhoods in San Francisco. The blocks just north were full of Victorians, churches, and music, and the nearby part of the Fillmore was well known for jazz, with the nickname the ["Harlem of the West,"](https://blackpast.org/african-american-history/fillmore-district-san-francisco/) where Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald played. In the 1950s the city labeled the area "blight." Through urban renewal, the Redevelopment Agency cleared it block by block, tore down thousands of Victorians, and moved out tens of thousands of residents, most of them Black. James Baldwin [called it](https://www.sfpublicpress.org/fillmore-revisited-how-redevelopment-tore-through-the-western-addition/) "Negro removal." The freeway came next. The double-deck [Central Freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/hayes-valley-the-central-freeway/) ran over the neighborhood, and the blocks under it were rough for years. I once heard someone call this stretch "murder valley." Into the early 1980s the area under the freeway was known for daytime prostitution and open drug sales. Then the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake [damaged the freeway](https://hoodline.com/2015/08/the-end-of-the-central-freeway-in-hayes-valley/). After a long fight it was torn down in 2003 and replaced with Octavia Boulevard, and Patricia's Green was built on the cleared land. The old neighborhood didn't come back. Prices went up, the boutiques moved in, and today's Hayes Valley grew on top of what was already gone. That also helps explain why some other neighborhoods feel more lived-in. The Mission has gentrified too, but it was never cleared this completely, so new people had to fit into what was already there. Hayes Valley didn't have much of that left. That history made me think about South Minneapolis (I grew up in the suburbs outside of Minneapolis). The Central part of Powderhorn became a mostly Black, middle-class neighborhood, and then in 1959 [Interstate 35W](https://www.segregationbydesign.com/minneapolis/powderhorn) cut through and removed about fifty square blocks of it. Same decade, same idea as the Central Freeway. Decades later, George Floyd was killed in that same neighborhood. It is easy to look at Patricia's Green and see only the last ten years: the Rivians, the Waymos, the cold brew. But most of what is there now started with planning decisions made more than sixty years ago. Hayes Valley is a reminder of the many decades it can take for the full implications of policy decisions like urban renewal to play out.
